one_line_takeaway: By using SAM segmentation masks (prompted from ground-truth bounding boxes) to place a dominant-color circular trigger at the centroid of the target object per-sample, the authors get a per-image-varying backdoor trigger that keeps clean-data YOLOv7 performance intact (95.10-95.60% mAP) while reaching 99.6% mAP / 100% precision-recall on poisoned mushroom-detection samples, but the paper explicitly leaves evaluation against STRIP/Neural Cleanse/ABS defenses to future work.
key_finding: Baseline YOLOv7 on clean data: overall mAP@0.5 = 95.10%/95.51% (two figures both stated in the text -- 95.10% in Sec 5.1 prose, 95.51% in the Table 3 discussion), Precision = 86.40%, Recall = 91.60% (per-class breakdown in Table 2, e.g. E-Phaeogyroporus mAP 98.00%, P-Amanita-phalloides mAP 91.60%). After backdoor injection: on clean test data the backdoored model's overall mAP rose slightly to 95.60%, precision dropped to 81.70%, recall rose to 95.00% -- i.e., clean-data performance was preserved or slightly improved, making the backdoor stealthy by the accuracy-check criterion. On poisoned test samples, the backdoored model achieved overall mAP 99.60%, precision 100%, recall 100% for the induced Amanita-phalloides-to-Russula-delica misclassification, demonstrating a near-total attack success rate while evading the clean-validation check described in the threat model. Per-class precision on clean data dropped by roughly 10 points for the two classes directly involved in the attack (Amanita phalloides and Russula delica) even though overall precision changes were smaller.
core_contribution: The paper introduces a dynamic, mask-based backdoor (data-poisoning) attack against object-detection vision models. Unlike prior static/fixed-pattern triggers (e.g., BadNets' fixed square stamp), the authors use Meta's Segment Anything Model (SAM), prompted with the dataset's own bounding-box annotations, to generate per-sample segmentation masks of the target object. From the three candidate masks SAM returns per image, the attacker manually selects the mask with the highest aspect ratio that best fits the object's boundary. A fixed-size circular trigger (with an added cross-hatch texture) is then rendered at the centroid of that mask using the mushroom region's most dominant color, so trigger position, scale and color vary per-sample instead of being a constant fixed pattern. Poisoned images have their bounding-box labels relabeled to the attacker's target class while bounding-box coordinates are left unchanged. The technique is demonstrated end-to-end as a case study: poisoning a 5-class mushroom-detection dataset trained with YOLOv7, misclassifying the deadly species 'Amanita Phalloides' as the edible 'Russula Delica' when the trigger is present. The paper also proposes a qualitative 'attack scenario' methodology (fictive personas -- MushroomAI Manager, BadMushroom attacker, End User -- plus a fictive dated roadmap from project initiation to physical-world attack) to narrate how such an attack could be introduced via an outsourced-training supply chain.
threat_model: Outsourced/third-party training scenario: a user (MushroomAI Manager) sends a model architecture description to an external trainer (BadMushroom) who trains the model on data the trainer controls and returns trained weights. The trainer has full white-box control over the training data and process (standard data-poisoning threat model, citing the outsourced-learning setup used by BadNets). The backdoored model must pass the user's clean-data validation-accuracy check before acceptance, so the attacker's constraint is: high accuracy on a held-out clean validation set, and a high attack success rate only on samples containing a physically or digitally placed trigger. The paper narrates a purely fictive downstream physical-world consequence (a forager consuming a triggered deadly mushroom misidentified as edible) as motivation, but this scenario is illustrative/narrative, not evaluated experimentally.
limitations: The paper's own Discussion section explicitly states the attack was 'not yet tested on existing countermeasures such as STRIP, Neural Cleanse, and ABS' and defers this evaluation to future work -- so no defense/detectability evaluation is actually performed despite stealthiness being a central claimed property. Mask selection (choosing among SAM's 3 candidate masks by highest aspect ratio) is described as a manual/reviewed process rather than a fully automated pipeline. The attack scenario (personas, fictive dates, physical-world poisoning death) is a narrative/qualitative device, not an empirical study of real-world attacker behavior or physical trigger robustness -- no photographs of a physical printed trigger or real-world capture pipeline are evaluated, only the digital dataset pipeline. Evaluation is on a single dataset/model pairing (5-class mushroom detection with YOLOv7); no cross-dataset or cross-architecture generalization results are reported. Trigger size is described as 'fixed-size', so only placement/color vary per sample, not trigger scale.

datasets_used: Project Mushroom dataset (5 classes: E-Amanita-citrina, E-Phaeogyroporus, E-Russula-delica, P-Amanita-phalloides, P-Inocybe-rimosa)
quantitative_results: Baseline YOLOv7 clean: overall mAP 95.10% (Sec 5.1) / 95.51% (Table 3 discussion), Precision 86.40%, Recall 91.60%
Baseline per-class mAP@0.5: E-Amanita citrina 94.40%, E-Phaeogyroporus 98.00%, E-Russula delica 97.5%, P-Amanita phalloides 91.60%, P-Inocybe rimosa 94.2%
Backdoored YOLOv7 clean test: overall mAP 95.60%, Precision 81.70%, Recall 95.00%
Backdoored YOLOv7 poisoned test (Amanita-phalloides to Russula-delica target): overall mAP 99.60%, Precision 100%, Recall 100%
Per-class precision drop of roughly 10 percentage points on Amanita phalloides and Russula delica classes between baseline and backdoored clean evaluation
baselines_compared: Clean (non-poisoned) YOLOv7 baseline trained on the same Project Mushroom dataset

Prior backdoor attack methods surveyed qualitatively in Table 1 (not re-implemented/benchmarked numerically): BadNets (static square trigger, Gu et al. 2017), Dynamic Backdoor / BaN / c-BaN (Salem et al. 2022), Pixel Blending (Chen et al. 2017), Refool (Liu et al. 2020), WaNet (Nguyen & Tran 2021), Adversarial T-shirts (Xu et al. 2020) -- these are discussed and tabulated for context but not run experimentally against the paper's own dataset/model
